title: Login redirect loop after SSO change
body:
We recently switched our company's SSO provider from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID. Since the switch, some users are getting stuck in an infinite redirect loop when trying to log into TaskFlow. They click login, get redirected to Microsoft, authenticate successfully, get redirected back to TaskFlow, and then immediately get sent back to Microsoft again. Not all users are affected — maybe 30% of our team. The rest can log in fine.

We've already verified that the Entra ID app registration is correct and matches our redirect URIs. The affected users can log into other apps that use the same SSO just fine.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: Thanks for the detailed write-up — that's a tricky one. I'd like to understand the pattern behind who's affected. Have you noticed anything that the ~30% of affected users have in common that distinguishes them from the ones who can log in fine? For example, are they in a particular group or role, did they join before/after a certain date, or are they using a different browser or device? Even a hunch would be helpful here.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Good question. I've been trying to figure out a pattern myself. One thing I noticed is that a few of the affected users have those plus-sign email addresses — like jane+taskflow@company.com — we set some people up that way for filtering purposes. But not all affected users have that, so I'm not sure it's the whole picture. I also think some of the affected people had existing TaskFlow accounts before we switched SSO providers, and their emails might have changed or been set up as aliases in Entra ID. I haven't checked every single person though, so I could be wrong about that second part. Browser-wise it seems to happen across Chrome and Edge at least.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's really helpful — the plus-sign addresses and the pre-existing accounts with alias changes both feel like they could be part of the puzzle. I'd like to follow that thread a bit further.

When an affected user hits this loop, are you able to see what's happening on TaskFlow's side? For instance, if you check TaskFlow's authentication logs (or the browser's network traffic during the redirect), can you see whether TaskFlow is receiving the identity token from Entra ID and then rejecting it — and if so, what the mismatch looks like? I'm wondering whether the email claim coming back from Entra ID differs from what TaskFlow has stored for those users (e.g., Entra sends `jane+taskflow@company.com` but TaskFlow expects `jane@company.com`, or vice versa).

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: I haven't dug too deep into the server-side logs honestly, but I did have one of our more technical users open the browser dev tools while it was happening. They said they could see the redirect coming back from Microsoft with what looked like a valid token, and there was a Set-Cookie header in the response, but the cookie didn't seem to stick — like it wasn't actually being saved by the browser. Then TaskFlow acts like the user isn't authenticated and sends them back to Microsoft again. As for the email mismatch thing, I think you might be onto something but in the opposite direction — I believe Entra sends the primary email WITHOUT the plus part, so like jane@company.com, but TaskFlow might have them stored as jane+taskflow@company.com from when we set them up originally. I'm not 100% sure on that though, I'd have to double-check what Entra is actually sending in the token.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for digging into that — this is starting to come into focus. Let me share where I think the pieces fit together, and you can tell me if this sounds right:

It seems like what might be happening is: the user authenticates successfully with Entra ID, Entra sends back a token with the primary email (e.g., `jane@company.com`), TaskFlow receives it and tries to match it to a local account, but some users are stored with their plus-sign address (`jane+taskflow@company.com`). When the lookup fails, TaskFlow never establishes a valid session — which is why the cookie appears to not stick. Without a session, TaskFlow treats the user as unauthenticated and kicks off the redirect again.

Does that narrative match what you're seeing? And one thing that would help confirm or rule this out: for the affected users who *don't* have plus-sign emails, is there anything else that might cause their email in Entra ID to differ from what's stored in TaskFlow — for example, were any of their email addresses updated or aliased during the SSO migration?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
That narrative makes sense for the plus-sign users, but I'm not sure it fully explains the cookie thing. The user who checked dev tools said the Set-Cookie header WAS there in the redirect response — like TaskFlow was trying to set a session cookie, it just wasn't being saved by the browser. So it seemed less like TaskFlow was refusing to create a session, and more like the session cookie was getting lost somewhere in the redirect. But I could be misunderstanding what they saw.

As for the second question — yes, actually, I think that's right. A few people had their email addresses changed when we moved to Entra ID. Like, they might have been bob@oldcompany.com in TaskFlow originally, and then in Entra their primary email is bob@company.com with the old one as an alias. So yeah, there could be a mismatch there too. I also noticed that some affected users can sometimes get in if they use an incognito window, which is weird if it's purely an email matching issue, right?

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
You're right to flag that — the incognito window thing is a really important clue, and it does suggest there's more going on than just email matching.

Incognito works because it starts with a completely clean slate — no existing cookies. That makes me wonder: do the affected users still have old cookies left over from when TaskFlow was connected to Okta? For example, if TaskFlow's session cookie name or domain didn't change between SSO providers, the browser might be sending a stale Okta-era session cookie alongside the new one that Entra is trying to set, and TaskFlow could be reading the old one instead.

Could you try something with one of the affected users? Have them clear just their TaskFlow cookies (not all cookies — just the ones for your TaskFlow domain) in their regular browser, and then try logging in again. If that fixes it, it would tell us the redirect loop is largely a stale-cookie problem. And if they still loop even after clearing cookies, that would confirm the email mismatch is the real driver.

Also — when they get in via incognito, does it keep working in incognito on subsequent visits, or do they hit the loop again next time they open an incognito window?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
Yeah, I can try the cookie clearing thing with someone tomorrow. I did actually have one person try clearing their cookies already — I think it worked for them briefly but then the loop came back on their next login attempt. So it wasn't a permanent fix. That's why I'm not sure it's just stale cookies from Okta.

As for incognito — I think it's hit or miss honestly. One person said it worked the first time but then looped again when they tried a second incognito window later. Another person said incognito worked consistently for them. So I'm not really sure what to make of that. It's not a reliable workaround unfortunately.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
This is really helpful — I think we're close to untangling this. The cookie behavior is the piece I keep coming back to, because it cuts across both the plus-sign users and the non-plus-sign users.

When you switched from Okta to Entra ID, did the redirect flow change in terms of domains? For example, with Okta the redirect might have gone `taskflow.company.com → okta.com → taskflow.company.com`, and now with Entra it might pass through `login.microsoftonline.com` or an intermediate page. What I'm getting at is whether the *path* the browser takes back to TaskFlow changed — because if there's now an extra redirect hop or a different subdomain involved, it can cause browsers to silently reject the session cookie due to cross-site protections (the `SameSite` cookie attribute).

Also — is TaskFlow accessed over HTTPS, and did anything about the domain or URL change during the migration (even something subtle like going from `taskflow.company.com` to `app.taskflow.company.com`)? A cookie marked `Secure` won't stick over plain HTTP, and a cookie scoped to one domain won't be sent to a different one — either of which could explain the exact symptoms you're describing.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Yeah, the redirect flow definitely changed. With Okta it was pretty straightforward — our TaskFlow instance redirects to Okta and back. Now with Entra it goes through login.microsoftonline.com. I don't think there's an extra hop or anything, but the domain is obviously different than Okta's was. TaskFlow is on HTTPS and the URL didn't change at all — still the same taskflow.company.com it's always been. The SameSite thing is interesting though — the user who checked dev tools did mention the Set-Cookie header was there but the cookie wasn't being saved. Could that be a SameSite issue? Like, because the redirect is coming back from Microsoft's domain, the browser is dropping the cookie? That would actually explain why incognito is inconsistent too, now that I think about it.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Infinite login redirect loop after Okta-to-Entra ID SSO migration due to SameSite cookie rejection and email claim mismatch","problem":"After migrating SSO from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID, approximately 30% of users are stuck in an infinite redirect loop: they authenticate successfully with Entra, get redirected back to TaskFlow with a valid token and Set-Cookie header, but the session cookie is not saved by the browser, causing TaskFlow to treat them as unauthenticated and redirect them back to Microsoft.","root_cause_hypothesis":"Two compounding issues: (1) TaskFlow's session cookie likely has SameSite=Lax or SameSite=Strict, which causes browsers to reject it when set during a cross-site redirect from login.microsoftonline.com. With Okta, the flow may have worked differently (e.g., different redirect mechanics or the cookie attribute wasn't an issue). This is the primary cause of the redirect loop. (2) For a subset of affected users, even if the cookie issue is resolved, an email claim mismatch will prevent session creation — Entra sends the primary email (e.g., jane@company.com) but TaskFlow has the user stored under a plus-sign variant (jane+taskflow@company.com) or a pre-migration domain (bob@oldcompany.com), causing the account lookup to fail.","reproduction_steps":["Have a user whose TaskFlow account was created with a plus-sign email (e.g., jane+taskflow@company.com) or a pre-migration email domain attempt to log in","User clicks login on taskflow.company.com and is redirected to login.microsoftonline.com","User authenticates successfully with Entra ID","User is redirected back to taskflow.company.com with a valid token","Observe in browser dev tools: Set-Cookie header is present in the response but the cookie is not persisted by the browser","TaskFlow treats the user as unauthenticated and redirects back to Microsoft, creating an infinite loop","Optionally verify: clearing cookies may temporarily fix it, incognito may work inconsistently"],"environment":"TaskFlow hosted at taskflow.company.com over HTTPS. SSO recently migrated from Okta to Microsoft Entra ID (login.microsoftonline.com). Issue occurs across Chrome and Edge. Affects ~30% of users, including those with plus-sign email addresses and those whose email domains changed during migration.","severity":"high","impact":"Approximately 30% of the team is completely unable to log into TaskFlow through normal means. There is no reliable workaround — incognito and cookie clearing are inconsistent. This blocks affected users from accessing the application entirely.","recommended_fix":"Address both root causes: (1) **Cookie fix (primary):** Inspect TaskFlow's session cookie attributes. Set `SameSite=None; Secure` on the session cookie so it is accepted during the cross-site redirect back from login.microsoftonline.com. Alternatively, implement a POST-based redirect landing page or an intermediate same-origin callback that sets the cookie outside of the cross-site redirect context. (2) **Email matching fix (secondary):** Update the user lookup logic during SSO authentication to normalize email addresses — strip plus-sign suffixes before matching, and/or match against known email aliases. Consider adding a migration script to reconcile stored emails with Entra ID primary emails for users whose domains changed (e.g., bob@oldcompany.com → bob@company.com).","proposed_test_case":"Create a test user in TaskFlow with a plus-sign email address (test+alias@company.com). Configure Entra ID to return the primary email without the plus-sign part. Initiate the SSO login flow and verify: (a) the session cookie is persisted by the browser after the cross-site redirect, (b) the user is matched to their TaskFlow account despite the email format difference, (c) the user lands on the authenticated dashboard without any redirect loop, and (d) subsequent login attempts in the same browser session also succeed without looping.","information_gaps":["Exact SameSite attribute value currently set on TaskFlow's session cookie — needs server-side inspection","Whether TaskFlow's auth code handles the token-to-session creation in the redirect response itself or via a subsequent same-origin request","Complete list of affected users cross-referenced with their stored email vs. Entra ID primary email to confirm the mismatch theory for non-plus-sign users","TaskFlow server-side authentication logs showing the exact point of failure (cookie rejection vs. account lookup failure) per user"]}
